title: Postmortem Care
subtitle: The nurse provides dignified care of the body after death and supports the grieving family — leaving tubes in place for medical-examiner cases, positioning before rigor, and honoring cultural and religious wishes.

patient.name: Postmortem Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse provides dignified postmortem care and supports the family after a patient's death.

nursesNote: After the death is pronounced and the time noted, the nurse provides postmortem care with dignity and respect. Before doing anything, the nurse confirms whether the death is a medical-examiner/autopsy case or involves organ or tissue donation — in those situations all tubes, lines, and devices are left in place. The body is cleaned, soiled dressings are replaced, and drainage pads are applied; the eyes are closed and dentures inserted to maintain facial appearance, and the body is positioned supine with the head slightly elevated to prevent facial discoloration. Because rigor mortis begins within 2-4 hours, the nurse positions the body before it sets in and applies identification tags per policy. Throughout, the nurse honors the family's cultural and religious practices, provides a private space for viewing and goodbyes, offers chaplaincy and bereavement resources, and documents belongings released to the family.
providerOrders: Note the time of death after pronouncement
Leave all tubes and lines in place for a medical-examiner, autopsy, or donation case
Position the body supine with the head slightly elevated before rigor sets in
Apply identification tags and document belongings per policy
Honor the family's cultural and religious wishes and offer support

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about postmortem care?

opts.text: Postmortem care includes dignity for the body and support for the family
The body is positioned supine with the head slightly elevated
In an autopsy or medical-examiner case, tubes and lines are left in place
Postmortem care should be rushed to free the room quickly
The eyes are closed and dentures inserted to maintain appearance
Cultural and religious practices should be respected
Elevating the head slightly helps prevent facial discoloration
The body should be positioned before rigor mortis sets in (2-4 h)
Identification tags are applied per facility policy
Family members should never be allowed to view the body
Belongings are documented and released to the family
Tubes are not removed when the medical examiner is involved
Verify the death has been pronounced and note the time
Determine if it is an autopsy/ME or donation case
Clean and position the body supine with the head slightly up
Apply identification tags per policy
Allow the family to view the body in private
Closing the eyes and inserting dentures to maintain facial shape
Removing all tubes before the medical examiner reviews the case
Positioning the body supine with the head slightly elevated
Leaving the body completely flat for hours
Applying identification tags per policy
Ignoring the family's religious practices
Cleaning the body and placing pads for drainage
Waiting to position the body until after rigor sets in

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Provide a private, quiet space
Offer simple, honest information and presence
Respect cultural and religious wishes
Allow time to view and say goodbye
Offer resources (chaplain, social work, bereavement)
Treating the body with dignity and privacy
Pulling lines in a medical-examiner case
Positioning the body before rigor mortis sets in
Rushing the family out to free the room
Honoring the family's religious rituals
Discarding belongings instead of documenting them
Applying two identification tags per policy
Leaving the body flat to discolor the face
